results.title: There is an AI code review bubble
results.content: suggest changes, and if the PR author approves, a commit is created with the suggested change. One issue with this flow it that's it doesn't run any tests on the change before it's actually in the PR branch, so... Really best for typos and other tiny changes.  Alternatively you actually, you know, \_collaborate\_ with the PR author, work it out, run tests locally and/or on another pushed branch, and someone then pushes a change directly to the PR.  The complaints about nitpicks slowing things down too much or breaking things sound like solo-hero devs who assume their god-like PRs should be effectively auto-approved because how could their code even contain problems... No wonder they love working with "Dr Flattery the Always Wrong Bot".  \(Hilarious name borrowed from Angela Collier) | | | [...] free to not like such tooling for whatever reason.  > The complaints about nitpicks slowing things down too much or breaking things sound like solo-hero devs who assume their god-like PRs should be effectively auto-approved because how could their code even contain problems... No wonder they love working with "Dr Flattery the Always Wrong Bot".  Did you maybe respond to the wrong person? I'm not sure how that relates to my comment at all. | | | |  |  | zenolijo 4 months ago  | root | parent | prev | next (javascript:void(0))   Naming comments can be very useful in code that gets read by a lot of people. It can make the process of understanding the code much quicker. On the other hand, if it's less important code or the renaming is not clearly an improvement it can be quite useless. But [...] is not clearly an improvement it can be quite useless. But I've met some developers who has the opinion of reviews as pointless and just say "this works, just approve it already" which can be very frustrating when it's a codebase with a lot of collaboration. | | | |  |  | daotoad 4 months ago  | root | parent | next (javascript:void(0))   Naming comments are useful when someone catches something like: 1. you are violating a previously agreed upon standard for naming things  2. inconsistent naming, eg some places you use "catalog ID" and other places you use "item ID" (using separate words and spaces here because case is irrelevant).  3. the name you chose makes it easy to conflate two or more concepts in your system  4. the name you chose calls into question whether you correctly

results.title: How can you identify and fix potential issues with your code, such as bad coding practices or errors that may not affect its functionality?
results.content:
I will encourage a team to hard code entire methods or even classes/modules for a brief period of time. Say, for example, you are launching a new application with limited functionality to a small set of users in order to get their feedback. I might white-list the usernames by adding them to a hardcoded hash and use that for authentication at initial launch. Simple. Fast. And good enough. Once we get user feedback and determine that we need to roll this out to a larger user base, we go back and implement a more robust authentication. That might be days, weeks, or even months away. Meanwhile, we optimized for feedback and learning rather than spending time on a robust feature we might never need. Hard coding some values was fine. This is true technical debt. We made a pragmatic step to get [...] I have one other example involving bond trading.

The off by one error resulted in the incorrect discount factor being used when pricing a bond. I was new and writing a new interface to the old logic. In my testing I found the off-by-one error. It resulted in an error in the fifth or sixth decimal place. No big deal - until my boss explained that the result was being multiplied by millions of dollars.

In fact the error accounted for a significant part of the divisions profits for the last few years.

Fixing the error immediately would have caused a significant disruption to the markets. It likely would have cost a billion dollars in losses not just to the company but to their customers. [...] Don't multitask

Multitasking is cool. Great. Awesome. Don't do it. Put aside all other tasks and focus on one task until it is finished or can not be pursued further. Multitasking is an accronym for wasting time. Switching between tasks wastes time on reloading all information and system. No multitasking means better productivity. Except when a boss is calling. “Talking to boss” value > coding value.

Head start notes

When interrupted write down all information to start task next time without discovering everything again. It may take up to months or years to go back. So notes are important. Save time.

Choose tasks for your zone level

results.title: Why Your AI-Generated Code Keeps Breaking (And How to Fix Your Process) - DEV Community
Bug fixes to official Live Code Bench
results.content:
### Fix 3: Deprecate hard-coded chat templates

The above two issues unfairly grade the model, but this issue directly breaks it: the incorrect application of custom chat templates. Models are sensitive to their training chat template and the specific system prompt used, so altering the template config can cause catastrophic degrade in their performance. Unfortunately, LCB manually writes the template for each model in a single file and applies the template to the model prompt through formatted strings at runtime. For example, this is the manual template for Qwen3 models:

```
SYSTEM_MESSAGE_CODEQWEN = ( f"<|im_start|>system\nYou are a helpful assistant <|im_end|>\n<|im_start|>user" )
``` [...] Looking at the data, we also noticed that some responses had no code, although the answer was enclosed within backticks ````` This led us to the second bug: the official LCB was just checking for enclosing backticks without confirming there was actual code within those. Sometimes there was just a comment, and the LCB script would just extract the comment while ignoring the actual code generated by the model.

Example highlighting how the official LCB script discarded actual code snippets from model responses

We also noticed that the official LCB repo hard-codes the chat templates instead of using the chat completions API. This is another source for errors in the evaluation pipeline.

## The Three Main Fixes

### Fix 1: Disable the problematic “—stop” flag that causes premature cutoff [...] Start your SubstackGet the app

Substack is the home for great culture
